data.name: Bart Verbruggen
data.description: Verbruggen, still only 23, has been first-choice since Euro 2024, with Ronald Koeman relaxed about his relative youth. “I’m not at all worried,” the manager said. “At Brighton he’s proving he can handle the highest level.” Brighton’s coach, Fabian Hürzeler, agrees, pointing to the keeper’s growing confidence. “There’s now an even stronger personality between the posts. Even without the ball, he’s becoming much more of a presence.” Verbruggen signed his first professional contract with NAC Breda, but never played in the Dutch Eredivisie, instead making his breakthrough at Anderlecht. Then came 2023’s £16.3m move to England, a record fee for a keeper moving from the Belgian top flight. His personal philosophy? “The most important thing is to be honest with yourself,” he told the Athletic last year. “Look in the mirror. See what you’re doing really well, see what you want to develop and work on it with a lot of joy, hard work and dedication. Life will give you back what you give it.”

data.name: Lutsharel Geertruida
data.description: In 2024 Geertruida appeared in a documentary about his life, speaking openly for the first time about growing up with a stutter. Up to then he had tried to hide it by avoiding media appearances, but said in the film: “Everyone knows that I find it difficult to speak in front of an audience, but just like on the pitch, I’m doing my best in this area to improve. I see this documentary as another step in the right direction. And hopefully I can inspire children and adults who, like me, struggle with stuttering.” He loses the stutter when rapping, releasing a track called Game Over in 2024, and collaborating with the rap star Dubbel T on the single On Top. He moved to Sunderland on loan last year with a £20m option to buy, and has been tracked by multiple Premier League clubs. Initially left out of the World Cup squad, he was called up when Jurriën Timber was ruled out with a groin injury.

data.name: Marten de Roon
data.description: When De Roon missed Euro 2024 with a knee injury it looked as if his last shot at a big international tournament had gone. But here he is, at 35, one of several surprise picks by Ronald Koeman. The defensive midfielder, who had a season with Middlesbrough before returning to his former club Atalanta in 2017, played his 436th game for the Serie A club in March, breaking their record for most appearances. The qualities he brings are wide-ranging: tactical intelligence, relentless running and a big personality, which extends to social media, where he’s known for posting self-deprecating memes, Photoshops and comedy skits. Among them was 2021’s comedy vignette filmed in the Atalanta club shop, where he was on hand to sign and pay for the first three "De Roon" replica shirts purchased over the counter. Nobody turned up before closing time, with De Roon seen doing a jigsaw and playing cards while he waited, saying: “I bet Messi never has to deal with this.” His X profile biog reads: “Summary of playing football in Italy: when we win I get the best table and the food for free. When we lose, my wife has to go and pick it up.”

data.name: Virgil van Dijk
data.description: Uefa has handed out its European Footballer of the Year award since 2011. Only once has the prestigious prize gone to a defender – in 2019, when Virgil van Dijk beat Lionel Messi and Cristiano Ronaldo. “I had to work hard for every step of the way – that's part of me and I'm very happy about that,” he said as he accepted the award. “I'm very proud, it's all credit to everyone that's helped me along the way.” The son of a Surinamese mother and Dutch father, he was initially criticised in the youth ranks at Willem II after being deemed too laid-back. Via FC Groningen, Celtic and Southampton, he arrived at Liverpool in 2018. He is a proper leader for club and country, even though in the Netherlands he still rarely gets the credit he deserves, being held in higher esteem in England. That’s not true back at Willem II, though, where they named a stand after him at their Sportpark Prinsenhoeve youth facility in Tilburg last year. The club said: “With this, his name is not only immortalised in the rich history of Willem II, but also emphatically linked to the development of youth football in the city.”

data.name: Nathan Aké
data.description: The left-footer spent some time undecided about his international identity. Born in the Dutch town of Voorburg to a Dutch mother and Ivorian father, Aké played for Netherlands youth teams, then in 2017 held several sets of talks with the then Côte d'Ivoire head coach, Marc Wilmots, about switching allegiance. But when Dick Advocaat became Netherlands manager, he swiftly called Aké up, giving him his first, binding international appearance. A product of the Feyenoord and Chelsea youth systems, he has won it all since joining Manchester City from Bournemouth in 2020. Like Stefan de Vrij, he’s a big pianist, having taken to it during the coronavirus pandemic. “In the first lockdown we had a lot of spare time, so I taught myself from a few apps,” he told the Telegraph. “At City, I’ve played in front of the guys once in Dortmund at the hotel. I got a good reaction, but I don’t like too many eyes on me. I actually played at my father’s funeral too, but I found it difficult – there were too many eyes on me. It’s different with football, you’re used to it, but with the piano your hands start to shake and then you lose the tempo and everything.”

data.name: Jan Paul van Hecke
data.description: AKA the Giant of Arnemuiden, a nickname honouring his time at his local amateur club in the Zeeland town of Arnemuiden. The robust centre-back was snapped up by Brighton from NAC Breda six years ago but his breakthrough took some time, via loan spells at Heerenveen and in the Championship with Blackburn. Now, though, he’s a Premier League regular, and last year won Brighton’s player of the year award and their players’ player of the year too. He is the nephew of former international Jan Poortvliet, who played in the 1978 World Cup final loss to Argentina. Van Hecke said in 2024: “My uncle is enjoying my journey, he said he is proud and he watches me every game. When I played against Ajax, winning away made him emotional, he was in tears … He told me all the stories from the World Cup he played in. He said they were robbed against Argentina!”

data.name: Justin Kluivert
data.description: It definitely helps, as a youngster making your way in the game, if your dad is a global star who has the phone numbers of top managers and players. It was a call between his dad, Patrick, and the Roma legend Francesco Totti that led to Kluivert’s 2018 move from Ajax to Roma. He spent five years on their books, though three of them were on loan at RB Leipzig, Nice and Valencia, before he was sold to Bournemouth in 2023. That was the moment his career really took off and he earned a place back in the national team, having first made his debut in 2018. “I’m proud to wear the shirt of the Netherlands again,” he said after the recall. “I feel so much better, both on and off the pitch, in England. I feel so happy playing for Bournemouth.” His serious injury over the winter made him a doubt for this summer, but Ronald Koeman always said he would include him if he could prove his fitness.

data.name: Ryan Gravenberch
data.description: Gravenberch signed a new long-term deal at Liverpool in March, reflecting his massive improvement at Anfield since joining from Bayern in 2023. Still only 24, his future looks bright – and much brighter than it had, for a time, in Munich. His father, Ryan Sr, revealed this season that his son struggled badly to adjust when he first arrived in Germany, which is why they moved out there to support him. “The football world is hard,” his dad said. “Ryan was only 21 at that time and had a lot of problems. The move to Liverpool was the best thing that could happen: he went from that hell to his heaven.” As a child he learned a lot from his brother, Danzell, who is eight years older. “When I was little I watched him play outside,” he told the Guardian in 2024, “and when we were a bit older I would go with him to play on the streets. He’s a big boy so it was difficult for me, but he toughened me up.” Danzell went on to have a nomadic football career of his own, playing for 13 clubs, while Ryan became Ajax’s youngest ever player when he made his debut aged 16 years and 130 days.

data.name: Wout Weghorst
data.description: Argentina won’t want to face the Netherlands at this World Cup. If it happens, Weghorst will be ready to go to war again. The 1.98m (6ft 6in) striker proved his value as a supersub during the last World Cup when he changed the game against Argentina, scoring twice and leading the Dutch to extra time. But all his aggression on the pitch and in the tunnel – ending with a war of words with Lionel Messi, who shouted “Qué mirás, bobo? Andá pa' allá, bobo” ("What are you looking at, fool? Go away, fool”) – left the Argentinians steaming. A real grafter, Weghorst climbed the football ladder, supported by his millionaire father, all the way to his dream club, Manchester United. But in his six months there on loan from Burnley in 2023 he managed only two goals in 31 appearances, none in the league. He earned an affectionate nickname at Old Trafford from his teammates, who called him Bobo, in honour of the Messi incident. He wasn’t a regular at Ajax last season, but Ronald Koeman will still be ready to use him from the bench to change games this summer.

data.name: Memphis Depay
data.description: Memphis initially played with the name Depay on the back of his shirt, but in 2012 decided to drop his father’s surname, because he had walked out on the family when his son was four. A difficult childhood has been reflected in sometimes erratic behaviour. He famously drew criticism from animal rights groups in 2020 when he posted photos of himself with a baby liger, a cross between a lion and a tiger. He’s also known for his fashion, his music career, his in-play white headband and his skill move, standing on the ball with both feet. He did it during a Corinthians game against Palmeiras last year, resulting in a mass brawl. Brazil’s FA later said the move would be a yellow card offence in future, because it represents “provocation to a rival, and disrespects the game”. The documentary With Both Feet showed another side of him, centred on the enormous willpower it took to recover from a cruciate ligament injury in 2019. After mixed spells in Europe, including Manchester United and Barcelona, he moved to Brazil in 2024. During the World Cup qualifiers he left his passport at home in Brazil, missed his flight and arrived late. Typical Memphis, said his critics. But he remains the national side’s all-time top scorer and a valuable part of this squad.

data.name: Cody Gakpo
data.description: Religion plays a big part in Gakpo’s life and career. He listens to gospel music in his car, with The Blessing by singer Eline Bakker among his favourites; he often wears an “I belong to Jesus” T-shirt under his top, as made famous by his football idol Kaká; he led a Bible group in the Dutch squad at the last World Cup and he prays before games. “If I can’t go to church, I call the pastor,” he told the Dutch paper NRC. “Faith gives me peace. And strength when I need it. I often read the Bible before I go to sleep. It is a kind of love letter that tells us how we should treat each other.” He joined Liverpool from PSV in 2023 for an initial £37m, and signed a new long-term deal last year. In his spare time, he’s a big reader. “I mainly read books about psychology, faith and mindset,” Gakpo told Algemeen Dagblad. “Black Box Thinking [by Matthew Syed] is about making mistakes … If you create a culture where admitting your mistakes is key, people will develop more. I try to translate this to the football pitch.”

data.name: Mats Wieffer
data.description: Wieffer’s call-up was a bit of a surprise. Despite having 14 caps he hadn’t featured in the previous four international windows. But his club manager, Fabian Hürzeler, was among his biggest cheerleaders for a recall, pointing out his huge versatility. “He has a really good physicality, a good understanding of the game … he's an absolute team player.” Wieffer signed from Feyenoord in 2024 for a reported £25.4m, making him the Eredivisie side’s record sale. He wasn’t that impressed when he first arrived in Brighton, though. “It was summer, but the weather was terrible: grey, rain, wind. I was in a hotel with my family and seriously thought: 'Where on earth have I ended up?'” he told the Dutch outlet tubantia.nl in March. “But my parents stayed a bit longer after I left, and the next day the weather was suddenly beautiful. They sent me photos and I thought: 'OK, this is quite a different city.'” He has grown to love the ice cream on the sea front, too. "They have the best ice creams in South England. Just as good as at Van der Poel in Hengelo, but much bigger. One scoop is enough."

data.name: Robin Roefs
data.description: The 23-year-old needed only one season as a first-choice keeper in the Eredivisie to secure a move to the Premier League. And shortly after that switch from NEC Nijmegen to Sunderland last year, Roefs was handed his first call-up by the national-team coach, Ronald Koeman. “It’s all happening so fast,” the keeper said after getting the call. “You could say I’m living in a fairytale.” He joined NEC at the age of 11 and, before breaking through as a professional, spent years coaching keepers at his old amateur club, Heeswijk. As he left his hometown club at such an early age they were not entitled to any compensation when he made the big transfer to England, but after the move Roefs went back to visit, and presented them with a cheque for €25,000. “Heeswijk is the club where it all started for me,” he said, “and I’m happy that I can now give something back.” The club said in response: “The majority of the money will go to the youth. The youth are the future. Robin’s a real role model, and one of our youth goalkeepers even received some training from Robin today. It was a truly wonderful day for us.”

data.name: Tijjani Reijnders
data.description: Reijnders’ parents were massive fans of the former Ajax great Tijani Babangida, so choosing what to name their baby was easy. His mum even made sure to go with the double j in Tijjani – the correct spelling of Babangida’s first name on his passport, rather than the version wrongly registered when Babangida moved to the Netherlands in 1991. Last year the two men finally met, when Babangida gave Reijnders a signed copy of his autobiography. Reijnders’ dad, Martin, was also a footballer, and these days manufactures tactical whiteboards, while Reijnders’ brother, Eliano, plays for the Indonesian club Persib Bandung and, thanks to his mum’s nationality, the Indonesian national team. Reijnders joined Manchester City for £46.5m from Milan in June last year – some journey from his old part-time job in his local Aldi, which he did while he was a youngster at Zwolle. In 2023 he said: "I used to stock the shelves, work behind the till and earn a few hundred euros a month … my time at Aldi was really good for me. I've seen both sides of the coin."

data.name: Micky van de Ven
data.description: One of the world’s fastest footballers. Van de Ven clocked a Premier League record top speed of 37.38km/h (23.23mph) against Brentford in January 2024 – and also occupies positions four and five in the top five. It didn’t always come naturally to him: at his first professional club, Volendam, he was nicknamed Tractor because of his awkward movement. “They thought at the time that I wasn’t good enough for professional football,” he said, “so I worked hard on my agility and speed.” He won the Europa League with Spurs last year but arrived at the World Cup after a difficult season battling relegation, and with his club future the subject of plenty of speculation. He wears the No 37 shirt at Spurs, explaining last year: “When I was 15, we had a close group of friends. One of our best friends became ill, and he sadly did not survive. When I was here at Spurs for my signing day my best friend was with me. There were the three of us back then … We saw that number [37] was available and it was a special number to our friend who passed away.”

data.name: Guus Til
data.description: Born in Zambia, Til and his family moved to Amsterdam when he was three. He was raised in AZ Alkmaar’s youth system and grew into a solid attacking midfielder who can also do a job up front. He’s an unconventional player and he has had his detractors – but he isn’t bothered by them. “I think it’s nonsense when people say I’m not good on the ball … And why does it have to look good anyway? I don’t consider myself a beautiful footballer; I’m quite gangly. Have you ever seen Medvedev play tennis? It looks like he has a fly-swatter in his hand. But he is one of the best tennis players in the world.” Speaking before his surprise call-up by Ronald Koeman, he told the newspaper de Volkskrant: “It is nice he is thinking about me. But if it happens, I don’t want it to look like I’m going along as a tourist. I want to go because I can play football well. I’m not going to sit on the bench just for the fun of it.” Whatever happens for Til this summer, don’t expect him to tell you about it: he really doesn’t like interviews. “No, I don’t like them, not at all. Others can talk about me, I’ll show it on the field.”

data.name: Noa Lang
data.description: It’s been a lively journey so far. The controversial winger has had six clubs in the last five seasons. His teammates in the national squad call him “trouble”. But Lang proved several times that he can play great football, and never lacks self-belief. When he left Belgium, where he played for Club Brugge, he said: “I made Belgian football hot. I honestly think so. Believe me: Belgium is going to miss me when I'm gone. They are going to pray there will be a new Noa Lang.’’ And in 2022, after a scoring display for the national side, he said: “Was I nervous? No. I play in the Dutch national team because I can play football well. Why should I be nervous about something I can do very well?” Asked if he had been encouraged to tone it down a bit, he said: “This is how I am. Noa Lang is being interviewed, then you will get Noa Lang … I like to just say what I think." This summer he will rival Cody Gakpo for the left-wing position.

data.name: Donyell Malen
data.description: Leaving Aston Villa for Roma on loan last year definitely paid off. Malen scored 10 goals in his first 12 Serie A games, matching Gabriel Batistuta’s record from 2000. “I am very proud of the comparison with Batistuta,” he said. “I’ve seen so many clips of him; he was a great player. My objective is to keep scoring more and more.” He made the move partly in a bid to keep his spot in the national team, because he was struggling for regular starts at Villa. He started out in Ajax’s youth system before moving to Arsenal in 2015, but returned to the Netherlands with PSV two years later. He moved to the Bundesliga with Borussia Dortmund in 2021, and to Villa, for £21m, last year. Ronald Koeman is likely to use Malen as his striker this summer if Memphis Depay struggles with injuries. If not, Malen will play on the wing. His personal philosophy: “I'm a forward. I try to create, score goals, give assists and just give the stadium a buzz. Try to do special things.”

data.name: Brian Brobbey
data.description: After a disappointing 2024-25 at Ajax, a place in this World Cup squad seemed miles away for Brobbey. But his £17.3m move to Sunderland changed all that, as he showcased his physicality, movement and ability to dominate Premier League defenders. He hasn’t always made the most clever career moves, though. Early on he left Ajax, tempted by RB Leipzig, but came back soon after. “It was a hard lesson for me,” he said. “If I had stayed at Ajax it would have been much better for my development.” His return didn’t go smoothly at first: goals were lacking, and then, in October 2023, he was involved in a horrible collision with the RKC Waalwijk goalkeeper Etienne Vaessen, who was left unconscious. The match was abandoned and Vaessen was out of action for weeks. When the final 10 minutes of the game were eventually played out in December that year, with Vaessen back in goal, the pair exchanged hugs. Vaessen said: “The frequent contact with Brian [during my recovery] did me and my family good. I didn't hold anything against him. He is a nice guy, and he gave me his shirt for my son. My son is a total Brobbey fan.”

data.name: Teun Koopmeiners
data.description: As children, Teun and his little brother, Peer, both loved the Koen Kampioen series of football books by the Dutch writer Fred Diks. Inspired by the stories, they both grew up to be professionals – Peer plays for AZ Alkmaar – and are now collaborating with Diks on their own series of football adventures. Teun will have plenty of stories to share. Before 2024’s £46m move from Atalanta to Juventus, where he has struggled at times and been criticised by fans, he was heavily linked with Liverpool. But Peer said last year that Teun had no regrets about the decision to stay in Italy rather than push for a move to England. “Teun loves Juve. As his brother, I can tell you I had never seen him as happy as he was in the photos he sent me on the day of his presentation. His face said it all – I had never seen that kind of happiness on him before.” Teun made his international debut in 2020 but missed Euro 2024 because of injury.

data.name: Frenkie de Jong
data.description: As a youngster in Ajax’s youth system, De Jong was once tasked with providing some opposition for first-team players during a training session. Instead of keeping his head down and sitting back, he started dribbling around them and scoring, until the coach shouted at him to stop. It was a glimpse of what was to come. Paris Saint-Germain, Manchester City and Manchester United all made moves for him before he left Ajax in 2019, but his heart was set on Barcelona. As a boy he and his family used to holiday 50 miles away from the city on a campsite near L’Escala. “Those holidays were great,” he recalled, soon after sealing the €75m transfer. “There were three football pitches on the campsite. One summer, we even went to watch a Barça friendly. I remember Ronaldinho playing and it was incredibly exciting to watch him live.” These days, he is one of the legends. He wears the No 21 shirt as a tribute to his grandfather. “I brought my whole family to my presentation in Barcelona,” he said in 2019, “except my grandfather Hans de Jong, my father's father. He died on the day of my 21st birthday. It is kind of a tribute. He was a huge football fan."

data.name: Denzel Dumfries
data.description: Named after the American actor Denzel Washington by his Aruban father and Surinamese mother, Dumfries spent many years playing for amateur clubs and was only signed by a professional team, Sparta Rotterdam, at the age of 18. He had to face down the doubters throughout his youth career. “It was always tough when they were laughing when I said I’d be a pro,” he told Sports Illustrated last year. “To be honest, I never thought I would reach this far … I’ve played at this level for a very long time now. It’s not something I was expecting when I was younger. But I knew I would be a professional football player, I knew.” The experience shaped his character, helped him build tremendous stamina and work rate, and brought him considerable success. With Inter he has amassed numerous major trophies, and he’s a key player for this Netherlands side. In 2014 he played two friendly matches for Aruba, but switched to the Dutch international setup two years later. In 2023, together with his family, he opened a specialist youth care and education centre in his home city of Rotterdam for young people needing specialist support outside mainstream education.

data.name: Mark Flekken
data.description: A real late bloomer. Flekken made his top-flight debut for Freiburg at the age of 25, after spending years playing in Germany’s lower divisions, where he once scored with a back-heel for Duisburg. At Freiburg he caught the eye of the then national-team coach, Louis van Gaal, in 2022, and while he didn’t make the cut for the World Cup that year, he was named second-choice for Euro 2024 by Ronald Koeman, having impressed in England after 2023’s £11m move to Brentford. He left London last year for Bayer Leverkusen, with Phil Giles, Brentford’s director of football, saying: “We were expecting Mark to be our goalkeeper for many more years, but … he made it clear that he was interested in moving closer to home and with a possibility of Champions League football.” He sustained a knee injury in January, then struggled for consistency on his return, admitting in April his form had been patchy. “I'm a prime example of our problem. My performances haven't been consistent. It doesn't have to be a 10 out of 10 in every game, or even an 8. But an average of 6.5 or 7, then you're a very good keeper.”

data.name: Crysencio Summerville
data.description: It was a grim season for West Ham, but a handful of players came out of it with credit and Summerville was among them. After signing from Leeds for £25m in August 2024, his first season was disrupted badly by a hamstring injury. But last season he was at the centre of many of the team’s better moments. When relegation was confirmed he posted online: "West Ham United is too big, too special, and too powerful to stay down for long. The comeback starts now." But whether he will be part of that comeback attempt remains to be seen; plenty of rival clubs have shown an interest. Born in Rotterdam, he joined Feynoord’s academy in 2008, and caught the eye during a loan spell at ADO Den Haag. Away from football, he’s a big basketball fan. “I love to play on a basketball court because I love watching NBA,” he said in 2024. “My favourite players are LeBron James and Stephen Curry, both all-time greats, and I always try to replicate their shots when I’m on court.”

data.name: Jorrel Hato
data.description: Long considered one of the brightest defensive prospects in Europe, Hato captained Ajax at 17 and moved to Chelsea last year for an initial fee of £35.5m. But he struggled to find his feet at first. “I would lie if I say the first couple of months were easy, because they weren't,” he said in May. “It was a difficult time, but the second half of the season you saw the better side of me. I’ve showed my qualities, the ones I showed in Ajax.” Liam Rosenior was a big fan during his brief time as Chelsea manager, saying: “I've been so impressed with his temperament, his physicality, technically. He is a joy to work with every day.” Hato is a big fan of Arsenal’s Jurriën Timber, a fellow Ajax product. “My coaches always told me that I needed to look up to Virgil van Dijk, but when I played in the under-18s, I always looked up to Jurriën Timber,” Hato said last year. “I played with him for six months before he left, and I learned so much from him. When I came into the first team, he was my mentor.”

data.name: Quinten Timber
data.description: Jurriën’s twin. It’s not actually that unusual for two brothers to play for the Netherlands – other examples include Frank and Ronald de Boer, Willy and René van der Kerkhof, Rob and Richard Witschge and, of course, the national coach, Ronald Koeman, and his now-assistant, Erwin. The Timber twins also have three older brothers: Dylan, a defender for the Dutch club VVV-Venlo and the Curaçao national team, plus Shamier and Chris. In 2019 Chris spoke about the issues watching the twins develop as young players. “I always had trouble telling them apart. I would say that Quinten had made a nice move, but my mother would say it was Jurriën. They also had the same hairstyle, which didn't help either. Now, one is a bit taller and the other has a slightly rounder head.” Quinten arrived at the World Cup after a difficult season when he fell out with the then Feyenoord coach, Robin van Persie, after refusing a new contract. But his move to Marseille in January allowed him to prove his readiness for this summer.
